candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces the Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), a novel framework that fuses ideas from Wasserstein GANs and Wasserstein Auto-Encoders to adaptively learn the intrinsic dimension of data which often lies on a lower-dimensional manifold. The approach adjusts the latent prior from a standard Gaussian to a generalized normal distribution (with a diagonal matrix having 0/1 entries) so that the encoder-generator pair recovers the true (intrinsic) data manifold. Theoretical guarantees are provided regarding consistency, generalization error bounds, and dimension estimation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: LWGAN modifies the standard GAN and VAE architectures by replacing the fixed latent Gaussian N(0, I) with a generalized version N(0, A) where A is a diagonal matrix whose rank reveals the intrinsic dimension. It fuses the benefits of WAE’s reconstruction and WGAN’s stable training via minimax optimization. A rank penalty is added to enforce model simplicity and proper latent dimension, and gradient penalty techniques are used to maintain Lipschitz continuity of the critic. Theoretical analysis and proofs establish the existence of optimal encoder-generator pairs and ensure consistency of the intrinsic dimension estimation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The paper validates the LWGAN framework on both synthetic and real-world datasets. Simulated experiments include datasets like the Swiss roll, S-curve, and a hyperplane scenario, each with known intrinsic dimensions. Real-world experiments are conducted on MNIST (with both individual digits and all digits) and the CelebA face dataset. The evaluations use metrics such as Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error. Additional bootstrap experiments quantify the uncertainty in the estimated intrinsic dimensions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The approach assumes deterministic encoder-generator mappings within a compact parameter space and requires standard assumptions such as Lipschitz continuity and sufficient network capacity. In addition, while it effectively learns the intrinsic dimension under the provided conditions, the reliance on a pre-specified network structure and potential computational complexity in training rank-aware networks are not fully addressed.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential future directions include extending the framework to handle stochastic generators (e.g., by incorporating extra noise), integrating the method into advanced GAN architectures like BigGAN for high-resolution image synthesis, and exploring applications in structural estimation and other fields where adaptive latent representation is beneficial.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Accelerating Convergence of Score-Based Diffusion Models, Provably
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Score-based diffusion models, while achieving remarkable empirical
performance, often suffer from low sampling speed, due to extensive function
evaluations needed during the sampling phase. Despite a flurry of recent
activities towards speeding up diffusion generative modeling in practice,
theoretical underpinnings for acceleration techniques remain severely limited.
In this paper, we design novel training-free algorithms to accelerate popular
deterministic (i.e., DDIM) and stochastic (i.e., DDPM) samplers. Our
accelerated deterministic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/{T}^2)$ with $T$ the
number of steps, improving upon the $O(1/T)$ rate for the DDIM sampler; and our
accelerated stochastic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/T)$, outperforming the
rate $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ for the DDPM sampler. The design of our algorithms
leverages insights from higher-order approximation, and shares similar
intuitions as popular high-order ODE solvers like the DPM-Solver-2. Our theory
accommodates $\ell_2$-accurate score estimates, and does not require
log-concavity or smoothness on the target distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces provably accelerated sampling algorithms for score‐based diffusion models. In particular, it offers a training‐free, deterministic sampler (an accelerated ODE-based method similar to an improved DDIM) and an accelerated stochastic sampler (improving upon the DDPM) with significantly better non-asymptotic convergence rates. The theoretical results show that the deterministic sampler achieves an improved iteration complexity (scaling with 1/√ε compared to 1/ε in the vanilla method) and the stochastic sampler achieves an iteration complexity of roughly 1/ε compared to 1/ε² in standard approaches.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The authors leverage higher-order ODE approximations, incorporating momentum-like corrective terms into the update rules. They derive new discretizations inspired by popular ODE solvers (such as the DPM-Solver family) and provide rigorous non-asymptotic convergence analysis based on ℓ2 score estimation accuracy without requiring strong assumptions such as log-concavity or smoothness. The methodology also involves careful decomposition of error terms, density ratio analyses, and Taylor expansions used to bound the differences between the accelerated methods and the original samplers.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental evaluation is performed using pre-trained score functions (e.g., provided by Huggingface diffusers) on image datasets such as CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Bedroom, and LSUN-Churches. The experiments compare the progress of the original DDIM/DDPM samplers versus the accelerated variants in terms of image quality (crispness and noise reduction) across different numbers of function evaluations (NFEs). The study primarily uses visual comparisons and convergence metrics (e.g., total variation distance) to validate the theoretical findings.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: While the theoretical guarantees are strong, the analysis depends on several technical assumptions including ℓ2-accurate score estimation and control over the Jacobian estimation errors. Moreover, the convergence bounds have dependencies on the problem dimension and logarithmic factors, which may be sub-optimal in high-dimensional settings. The experimental validation focused mainly on the deterministic sampler, leaving the stochastic variant less explored in practice.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential future work includes refining the theoretical dependency on the dimension, exploring higher-order ODE solvers (such as third-order methods) for further acceleration, and extending the acceleration ideas to SDE-based samplers. There is also scope for investigating practical implementations that combine these accelerated methods with additional training or optimization strategies to further improve sampling speed and fidelity.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Imitation learning addresses the challenge of learning by observing an
expert's demonstrations without access to reward signals from environments.
Most existing imitation learning methods that do not require interacting with
environments either model the expert distribution as the conditional
probability p(a|s) (e.g., behavioral cloning, BC) or the joint probability p(s,
a). Despite the simplicity of modeling the conditional probability with BC, it
usually struggles with generalization. While modeling the joint probability can
improve generalization performance, the inference procedure is often
time-consuming, and the model can suffer from manifold overfitting. This work
proposes an imitation learning framework that benefits from modeling both the
conditional and joint probability of the expert distribution. Our proposed
Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning (DBC) employs a diffusion model
trained to model expert behaviors and learns a policy to optimize both the BC
loss (conditional) and our proposed diffusion model loss (joint). DBC
outperforms baselines in various continuous control tasks in navigation, robot
arm manipulation, dexterous manipulation, and locomotion. We design additional
experiments to verify the limitations of modeling either the conditional
probability or the joint probability of the expert distribution, as well as
compare different generative models. Ablation studies justify the effectiveness
of our design choices.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning (DBC), an imitation learning framework that combines the efficiency of behavioral cloning (modeling conditional probability p(a|s)) with the enhanced generalization of modeling the joint probability p(s, a) using diffusion models. This dual approach helps to mitigate overfitting and improves performance on several continuous control tasks compared to existing baselines.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The method augments traditional behavioral cloning by training a diffusion model (specifically a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model) on expert state-action pairs to capture their joint distribution. The resulting diffusion loss is combined with the BC loss (weighted via a hyperparameter λ) to guide policy learning. The technique leverages the diffusion model’s ability to denoise noisy inputs as an estimate of how well a predicted state-action pair fits the expert distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments were conducted in diverse continuous control domains including navigation (MAZE), robot arm manipulation (FETCH PICK), dexterous manipulation (HAND ROTATE), and locomotion tasks (CHEETAH, WALKER), as well as a combined locomotion and navigation task (ANTREACH). The framework is evaluated against baselines such as BC, Implicit BC, and Diffusion Policy using metrics like success rates and returns. Ablation studies and comparisons with alternative generative models (EBM, VAE, GAN) under different dataset sizes and noise levels further validate the approach.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method is limited to learning from expert demonstrations without interaction, meaning it cannot leverage agent-generated data. It also faces challenges related to manifold overfitting when high-dimensional data lies on a low-dimensional manifold and is inherently dependent on the quality and bias within the expert data. Additionally, optimal hyperparameter tuning (e.g., balancing BC loss and diffusion loss) is task-dependent.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could extend the framework by integrating agent trajectories and interactive learning scenarios to further refine policy performance. Other research avenues include addressing manifold overfitting more robustly, exploring different diffusion model architectures or integration with online reinforcement learning, and mitigating biases inherited from expert demonstrations.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: FiT: Flexible Vision Transformer for Diffusion Model
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Nature is infinitely resolution-free. In the context of this reality,
existing diffusion models, such as Diffusion Transformers, often face
challenges when processing image resolutions outside of their trained domain.
To overcome this limitation, we present the Flexible Vision Transformer (FiT),
a transformer architecture specifically designed for generating images with
unrestricted resolutions and aspect ratios. Unlike traditional methods that
perceive images as static-resolution grids, FiT conceptualizes images as
sequences of dynamically-sized tokens. This perspective enables a flexible
training strategy that effortlessly adapts to diverse aspect ratios during both
training and inference phases, thus promoting resolution generalization and
eliminating biases induced by image cropping. Enhanced by a meticulously
adjusted network structure and the integration of training-free extrapolation
techniques, FiT exhibits remarkable flexibility in resolution extrapolation
generation. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the exceptional performance
of FiT across a broad range of resolutions, showcasing its effectiveness both
within and beyond its training resolution distribution. Repository available at
https://github.com/whlzy/FiT.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces FiT, a Flexible Vision Transformer for diffusion models that generates images of arbitrary resolutions and aspect ratios. It overcomes the limitations of fixed-resolution training in previous models by leveraging a dynamic token sequence representation and a flexible training pipeline, achieving state-of-the-art extrapolation performance across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution resolutions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: FiT employs a modified transformer architecture based on DiT, incorporating innovations such as a flexible training pipeline that preserves the original image aspect ratio, dynamic token sequence handling with padding, Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention to manage variable token lengths, and 2D Rotary Positional Embedding (2D RoPE) for effective positional encoding. It also introduces training-free resolution extrapolation techniques (VisionNTK and VisionYaRN) to enhance performance at arbitrary resolutions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments are conducted on the ImageNet dataset with evaluations on both in-distribution resolutions (256×256, 160×320, 128×384) and out-of-distribution resolutions (320×320, 224×448, 160×480). Performance metrics include FID, sFID, Inception Score (IS), precision, and recall. Extensive ablation studies are also performed to assess architectural modifications and extrapolation techniques.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: FiT-XL/2 shows slightly inferior performance on standard 256×256 resolution compared to models trained with longer steps, and its capabilities at higher resolutions (larger token length limits) are not fully explored. Additionally, the study only considers training-free resolution extrapolation methods due to computational constraints, leaving other potentially effective approaches unexamined.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could involve training FiT with higher resolutions and more extensive token length configurations, exploring alternative and possibly training-based resolution extrapolation methods, and extending the framework to other generative tasks. Further investigations into enhancing performance at both standard and extreme resolutions may also be pursued.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: DiffDA: a Diffusion Model for Weather-scale Data Assimilation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
The generation of initial conditions via accurate data assimilation is
crucial for weather forecasting and climate modeling. We propose DiffDA as a
denoising diffusion model capable of assimilating atmospheric variables using
predicted states and sparse observations. Acknowledging the similarity between
a weather forecast model and a denoising diffusion model dedicated to weather
applications, we adapt the pretrained GraphCast neural network as the backbone
of the diffusion model. Through experiments based on simulated observations
from the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, our method can produce assimilated global
atmospheric data consistent with observations at 0.25 deg (~30km) resolution
globally. This marks the highest resolution achieved by ML data assimilation
models. The experiments also show that the initial conditions assimilated from
sparse observations (less than 0.96% of gridded data) and 48-hour forecast can
be used for forecast models with a loss of lead time of at most 24 hours
compared to initial conditions from state-of-the-art data assimilation in ERA5.
This enables the application of the method to real-world applications, such as
creating reanalysis datasets with autoregressive data assimilation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DiffDA, a novel ML-based data assimilation method that leverages a denoising diffusion probabilistic model to generate high-resolution atmospheric data (0.25° horizontal resolution) for weather forecasting and reanalysis. It integrates a pretrained GraphCast forecast model as its backbone and demonstrates that assimilated initial conditions from sparse observations combined with forecasts can achieve competitive accuracy with reduced computational cost.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: DiffDA is built upon the denoising diffusion probabilistic model framework. It conditions the diffusion process on both the predicted state (by integrating the forecast model’s output into the neural network) and sparse observational data. Conditioning for the sparse observations is applied during inference using a soft mask technique (softbleeding with a Gaussian kernel and interpolation via universal kriging) to merge known values with the diffusion output. The approach is implemented by adapting the architecture of the GraphCast model to align with the diffusion model’s input/output signatures.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments are conducted on the WeatherBench2 dataset, which is derived from ERA5 reanalysis data. The study includes multiple experiment settings: single-step data assimilation, autoregressive assimilation cycles (where assimilated data is used iteratively for forecasts), and forecasts based on assimilated data. Metrics such as RMSE for geopotential, temperature, humidity, and wind components are used for evaluation. Ablation studies on soft mask parameters (e.g., the Gaussian kernel standard deviation) further validate the model design.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method currently only supports point measurements at a single time step and does not incorporate other types of observations like satellite imagery or radar. It also relies on simulated observations from ERA5 for training and enforcement, and shows challenges in terms of error accumulation in autoregressive cycles and lack of built-in quality control for input data.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work includes extending the approach to handle diverse observation types (e.g., satellite imagery, radar), incorporating multi-time-step conditioning to facilitate 4-dimensional assimilation, developing quality control mechanisms for input data, and further stabilizing the model during long autoregressive assimilation cycles.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces the first exact framework for training diffusion models solely using corrupted (noisy) data. It provides a novel method that applies Tweedie’s formula twice to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above the training noise level, and employs a consistency loss to extend this capability to noise levels below. Additionally, it presents evidence that diffusion models exhibit significant memorization of training data and proposes an approach to mitigate dataset replication.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The proposed method combines an ambient denoising score matching objective with a consistency loss. The key technical contributions include a computationally efficient objective that avoids the divergence term by leveraging a double application of Tweedie’s formula, and a consistency loss that enforces the learned denoiser to be stable across different noise levels. This enables training to recover the uncorrupted distribution from noisy samples. The approach is theoretically justified through several lemmas and theorems, and is implemented by fine-tuning established models such as Stable Diffusion XL using corrupted latent representations.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments are conducted on multiple datasets including a subset of LAION images (using masking and high-noise corruption) and the FFHQ dataset, with additional experiments on chest x-rays. The study evaluates both denoising performance (using latent mean squared error and pixel MSE) and generation quality (using FID scores). The replication/memorization behavior is measured via a novel attack using DINOv2 embeddings and nearest neighbor similarity metrics, comparing models trained on clean versus corrupted data.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not address training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data, and the introduction of the consistency loss increases training time significantly. Moreover, preliminary experiments indicate that the ambient denoising score matching objective may fail when applied to very limited datasets (fewer than 100 samples).
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential future directions include extending the framework to handle linearly corrupted data, optimizing and reducing the computational overhead associated with the consistency loss, and further exploring the implications of model memorization in diffusion models. Additionally, applying these techniques to other domains such as MRI or black-hole imaging, and investigating other mitigation strategies for training data replication, present promising avenues for future work.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
TERD: A Unified Framework for Safeguarding Diffusion Models Against
  Backdoors
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have achieved notable success in image generation, but they
remain highly vulnerable to backdoor attacks, which compromise their integrity
by producing specific undesirable outputs when presented with a pre-defined
trigger. In this paper, we investigate how to protect diffusion models from
this dangerous threat. Specifically, we propose TERD, a backdoor defense
framework that builds unified modeling for current attacks, which enables us to
derive an accessible reversed loss. A trigger reversion strategy is further
employed: an initial approximation of the trigger through noise sampled from a
prior distribution, followed by refinement through differential multi-step
samplers. Additionally, with the reversed trigger, we propose backdoor
detection from the noise space, introducing the first backdoor input detection
approach for diffusion models and a novel model detection algorithm that
calculates the KL divergence between reversed and benign distributions.
Extensive evaluations demonstrate that TERD secures a 100% True Positive Rate
(TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) across datasets of varying resolutions. TERD
also demonstrates nice adaptability to other Stochastic Differential Equation
(SDE)-based models. Our code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ML/TERD.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces TERD, a unified framework to defend diffusion models against backdoor attacks. Its main contributions include a novel unified loss formulation for trigger reversion, a two-stage trigger estimation and refinement algorithm, and new detection methods that work in the noise space for both input and model protection.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: TERD first characterizes various backdoor attack formulations under a unified model, enabling the derivation of a reversed loss function. The approach comprises two stages: initial trigger estimation using noise sampled from a surrogate distribution and subsequent trigger refinement through differential multi-step sampling. Additionally, the method employs input detection by comparing probabilities in benign and backdoor noise distributions, and model detection using KL divergence metrics between reversed and benign triggers.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 as well as high-resolution datasets like CelebA and CelebA-HQ. The evaluation is performed against several established backdoor attacks (BadDiffusion, TrojDiff, VillanDiffusion) and spans diverse settings in terms of trigger sizes, poison rates, and adaptive attack scenarios. Metrics include the l2 norm between the reversed and original trigger, True Positive Rate (TPR), and True Negative Rate (TNR), with comparisons made against a baseline method.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: While TERD shows high efficacy, some limitations include potential degradation in performance under extremely low trigger scales (adaptive attacks) and the assumption that certain attack parameters conform to the unified formulation. The solution may also have issues when faced with extremely subtle triggers that closely resemble benign noise, and the overall defense relies on access to key model dynamics which may not always be feasible.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could extend the framework to other types of generative models beyond diffusion and SDE-based systems, explore more robust detection mechanisms for adaptive backdoor attacks, improve the efficiency of the reverse engineering steps, and study the integration of TERD in real-time, large-scale, and diverse practical deployment scenarios.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces the Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), a novel framework that fuses ideas from Wasserstein GANs and Wasserstein Auto-Encoders to adaptively learn the intrinsic dimension of data which often lies on a lower-dimensional manifold. The approach adjusts the latent prior from a standard Gaussian to a generalized normal distribution (with a diagonal matrix having 0/1 entries) so that the encoder-generator pair recovers the true (intrinsic) data manifold. Theoretical guarantees are provided regarding consistency, generalization error bounds, and dimension estimation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: LWGAN modifies the standard GAN and VAE architectures by replacing the fixed latent Gaussian N(0, I) with a generalized version N(0, A) where A is a diagonal matrix whose rank reveals the intrinsic dimension. It fuses the benefits of WAE’s reconstruction and WGAN’s stable training via minimax optimization. A rank penalty is added to enforce model simplicity and proper latent dimension, and gradient penalty techniques are used to maintain Lipschitz continuity of the critic. Theoretical analysis and proofs establish the existence of optimal encoder-generator pairs and ensure consistency of the intrinsic dimension estimation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The paper validates the LWGAN framework on both synthetic and real-world datasets. Simulated experiments include datasets like the Swiss roll, S-curve, and a hyperplane scenario, each with known intrinsic dimensions. Real-world experiments are conducted on MNIST (with both individual digits and all digits) and the CelebA face dataset. The evaluations use metrics such as Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error. Additional bootstrap experiments quantify the uncertainty in the estimated intrinsic dimensions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The approach assumes deterministic encoder-generator mappings within a compact parameter space and requires standard assumptions such as Lipschitz continuity and sufficient network capacity. In addition, while it effectively learns the intrinsic dimension under the provided conditions, the reliance on a pre-specified network structure and potential computational complexity in training rank-aware networks are not fully addressed.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential future directions include extending the framework to handle stochastic generators (e.g., by incorporating extra noise), integrating the method into advanced GAN architectures like BigGAN for high-resolution image synthesis, and exploring applications in structural estimation and other fields where adaptive latent representation is beneficial.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Accelerating Convergence of Score-Based Diffusion Models, Provably
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Score-based diffusion models, while achieving remarkable empirical
performance, often suffer from low sampling speed, due to extensive function
evaluations needed during the sampling phase. Despite a flurry of recent
activities towards speeding up diffusion generative modeling in practice,
theoretical underpinnings for acceleration techniques remain severely limited.
In this paper, we design novel training-free algorithms to accelerate popular
deterministic (i.e., DDIM) and stochastic (i.e., DDPM) samplers. Our
accelerated deterministic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/{T}^2)$ with $T$ the
number of steps, improving upon the $O(1/T)$ rate for the DDIM sampler; and our
accelerated stochastic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/T)$, outperforming the
rate $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ for the DDPM sampler. The design of our algorithms
leverages insights from higher-order approximation, and shares similar
intuitions as popular high-order ODE solvers like the DPM-Solver-2. Our theory
accommodates $\ell_2$-accurate score estimates, and does not require
log-concavity or smoothness on the target distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces provably accelerated sampling algorithms for score‐based diffusion models. In particular, it offers a training‐free, deterministic sampler (an accelerated ODE-based method similar to an improved DDIM) and an accelerated stochastic sampler (improving upon the DDPM) with significantly better non-asymptotic convergence rates. The theoretical results show that the deterministic sampler achieves an improved iteration complexity (scaling with 1/√ε compared to 1/ε in the vanilla method) and the stochastic sampler achieves an iteration complexity of roughly 1/ε compared to 1/ε² in standard approaches.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The authors leverage higher-order ODE approximations, incorporating momentum-like corrective terms into the update rules. They derive new discretizations inspired by popular ODE solvers (such as the DPM-Solver family) and provide rigorous non-asymptotic convergence analysis based on ℓ2 score estimation accuracy without requiring strong assumptions such as log-concavity or smoothness. The methodology also involves careful decomposition of error terms, density ratio analyses, and Taylor expansions used to bound the differences between the accelerated methods and the original samplers.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental evaluation is performed using pre-trained score functions (e.g., provided by Huggingface diffusers) on image datasets such as CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Bedroom, and LSUN-Churches. The experiments compare the progress of the original DDIM/DDPM samplers versus the accelerated variants in terms of image quality (crispness and noise reduction) across different numbers of function evaluations (NFEs). The study primarily uses visual comparisons and convergence metrics (e.g., total variation distance) to validate the theoretical findings.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: While the theoretical guarantees are strong, the analysis depends on several technical assumptions including ℓ2-accurate score estimation and control over the Jacobian estimation errors. Moreover, the convergence bounds have dependencies on the problem dimension and logarithmic factors, which may be sub-optimal in high-dimensional settings. The experimental validation focused mainly on the deterministic sampler, leaving the stochastic variant less explored in practice.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential future work includes refining the theoretical dependency on the dimension, exploring higher-order ODE solvers (such as third-order methods) for further acceleration, and extending the acceleration ideas to SDE-based samplers. There is also scope for investigating practical implementations that combine these accelerated methods with additional training or optimization strategies to further improve sampling speed and fidelity.

be used for forecast models with a loss of lead time of at most 24 hours
compared to initial conditions from state-of-the-art data assimilation in ERA5.
This enables the application of the method to real-world applications, such as
creating reanalysis datasets with autoregressive data assimilation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DiffDA, a novel ML-based data assimilation method that leverages a denoising diffusion probabilistic model to generate high-resolution atmospheric data (0.25° horizontal resolution) for weather forecasting and reanalysis. It integrates a pretrained GraphCast forecast model as its backbone and demonstrates that assimilated initial conditions from sparse observations combined with forecasts can achieve competitive accuracy with reduced computational cost.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: DiffDA is built upon the denoising diffusion probabilistic model framework. It conditions the diffusion process on both the predicted state (by integrating the forecast model’s output into the neural network) and sparse observational data. Conditioning for the sparse observations is applied during inference using a soft mask technique (softbleeding with a Gaussian kernel and interpolation via universal kriging) to merge known values with the diffusion output. The approach is implemented by adapting the architecture of the GraphCast model to align with the diffusion model’s input/output signatures.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments are conducted on the WeatherBench2 dataset, which is derived from ERA5 reanalysis data. The study includes multiple experiment settings: single-step data assimilation, autoregressive assimilation cycles (where assimilated data is used iteratively for forecasts), and forecasts based on assimilated data. Metrics such as RMSE for geopotential, temperature, humidity, and wind components are used for evaluation. Ablation studies on soft mask parameters (e.g., the Gaussian kernel standard deviation) further validate the model design.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method currently only supports point measurements at a single time step and does not incorporate other types of observations like satellite imagery or radar. It also relies on simulated observations from ERA5 for training and enforcement, and shows challenges in terms of error accumulation in autoregressive cycles and lack of built-in quality control for input data.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work includes extending the approach to handle diverse observation types (e.g., satellite imagery, radar), incorporating multi-time-step conditioning to facilitate 4-dimensional assimilation, developing quality control mechanisms for input data, and further stabilizing the model during long autoregressive assimilation cycles.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces the first exact framework for training diffusion models solely using corrupted (noisy) data. It provides a novel method that applies Tweedie’s formula twice to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above the training noise level, and employs a consistency loss to extend this capability to noise levels below. Additionally, it presents evidence that diffusion models exhibit significant memorization of training data and proposes an approach to mitigate dataset replication.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The proposed method combines an ambient denoising score matching objective with a consistency loss. The key technical contributions include a computationally efficient objective that avoids the divergence term by leveraging a double application of Tweedie’s formula, and a consistency loss that enforces the learned denoiser to be stable across different noise levels. This enables training to recover the uncorrupted distribution from noisy samples. The approach is theoretically justified through several lemmas and theorems, and is implemented by fine-tuning established models such as Stable Diffusion XL using corrupted latent representations.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments are conducted on multiple datasets including a subset of LAION images (using masking and high-noise corruption) and the FFHQ dataset, with additional experiments on chest x-rays. The study evaluates both denoising performance (using latent mean squared error and pixel MSE) and generation quality (using FID scores). The replication/memorization behavior is measured via a novel attack using DINOv2 embeddings and nearest neighbor similarity metrics, comparing models trained on clean versus corrupted data.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not address training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data, and the introduction of the consistency loss increases training time significantly. Moreover, preliminary experiments indicate that the ambient denoising score matching objective may fail when applied to very limited datasets (fewer than 100 samples).
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential future directions include extending the framework to handle linearly corrupted data, optimizing and reducing the computational overhead associated with the consistency loss, and further exploring the implications of model memorization in diffusion models. Additionally, applying these techniques to other domains such as MRI or black-hole imaging, and investigating other mitigation strategies for training data replication, present promising avenues for future work.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
TERD: A Unified Framework for Safeguarding Diffusion Models Against
  Backdoors
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have achieved notable success in image generation, but they
remain highly vulnerable to backdoor attacks, which compromise their integrity
by producing specific undesirable outputs when presented with a pre-defined
trigger. In this paper, we investigate how to protect diffusion models from
this dangerous threat. Specifically, we propose TERD, a backdoor defense
framework that builds unified modeling for current attacks, which enables us to
derive an accessible reversed loss. A trigger reversion strategy is further
employed: an initial approximation of the trigger through noise sampled from a
prior distribution, followed by refinement through differential multi-step
samplers. Additionally, with the reversed trigger, we propose backdoor
detection from the noise space, introducing the first backdoor input detection
approach for diffusion models and a novel model detection algorithm that
calculates the KL divergence between reversed and benign distributions.
Extensive evaluations demonstrate that TERD secures a 100% True Positive Rate
(TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) across datasets of varying resolutions. TERD
also demonstrates nice adaptability to other Stochastic Differential Equation
(SDE)-based models. Our code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ML/TERD.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces TERD, a unified framework to defend diffusion models against backdoor attacks. Its main contributions include a novel unified loss formulation for trigger reversion, a two-stage trigger estimation and refinement algorithm, and new detection methods that work in the noise space for both input and model protection.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: TERD first characterizes various backdoor attack formulations under a unified model, enabling the derivation of a reversed loss function. The approach comprises two stages: initial trigger estimation using noise sampled from a surrogate distribution and subsequent trigger refinement through differential multi-step sampling. Additionally, the method employs input detection by comparing probabilities in benign and backdoor noise distributions, and model detection using KL divergence metrics between reversed and benign triggers.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 as well as high-resolution datasets like CelebA and CelebA-HQ. The evaluation is performed against several established backdoor attacks (BadDiffusion, TrojDiff, VillanDiffusion) and spans diverse settings in terms of trigger sizes, poison rates, and adaptive attack scenarios. Metrics include the l2 norm between the reversed and original trigger, True Positive Rate (TPR), and True Negative Rate (TNR), with comparisons made against a baseline method.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: While TERD shows high efficacy, some limitations include potential degradation in performance under extremely low trigger scales (adaptive attacks) and the assumption that certain attack parameters conform to the unified formulation. The solution may also have issues when faced with extremely subtle triggers that closely resemble benign noise, and the overall defense relies on access to key model dynamics which may not always be feasible.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could extend the framework to other types of generative models beyond diffusion and SDE-based systems, explore more robust detection mechanisms for adaptive backdoor attacks, improve the efficiency of the reverse engineering steps, and study the integration of TERD in real-time, large-scale, and diverse practical deployment scenarios.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title: Accelerating Convergence of Score-Based Diffusion Models, Provably
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Score-based diffusion models, while achieving remarkable empirical
performance, often suffer from low sampling speed, due to extensive function
evaluations needed during the sampling phase. Despite a flurry of recent
activities towards speeding up diffusion generative modeling in practice,
theoretical underpinnings for acceleration techniques remain severely limited.
In this paper, we design novel training-free algorithms to accelerate popular
deterministic (i.e., DDIM) and stochastic (i.e., DDPM) samplers. Our
accelerated deterministic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/{T}^2)$ with $T$ the
number of steps, improving upon the $O(1/T)$ rate for the DDIM sampler; and our
accelerated stochastic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/T)$, outperforming the
rate $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ for the DDPM sampler. The design of our algorithms
leverages insights from higher-order approximation, and shares similar
intuitions as popular high-order ODE solvers like the DPM-Solver-2. Our theory
accommodates $\ell_2$-accurate score estimates, and does not require
log-concavity or smoothness on the target distribution.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces provably accelerated sampling algorithms for score‐based diffusion models. In particular, it offers a training‐free, deterministic sampler (an accelerated ODE-based method similar to an improved DDIM) and an accelerated stochastic sampler (improving upon the DDPM) with significantly better non-asymptotic convergence rates. The theoretical results show that the deterministic sampler achieves an improved iteration complexity (scaling with 1/√ε compared to 1/ε in the vanilla method) and the stochastic sampler achieves an iteration complexity of roughly 1/ε compared to 1/ε² in standard approaches.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The authors leverage higher-order ODE approximations, incorporating momentum-like corrective terms into the update rules. They derive new discretizations inspired by popular ODE solvers (such as the DPM-Solver family) and provide rigorous non-asymptotic convergence analysis based on ℓ2 score estimation accuracy without requiring strong assumptions such as log-concavity or smoothness. The methodology also involves careful decomposition of error terms, density ratio analyses, and Taylor expansions used to bound the differences between the accelerated methods and the original samplers.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental evaluation is performed using pre-trained score functions (e.g., provided by Huggingface diffusers) on image datasets such as CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Bedroom, and LSUN-Churches. The experiments compare the progress of the original DDIM/DDPM samplers versus the accelerated variants in terms of image quality (crispness and noise reduction) across different numbers of function evaluations (NFEs). The study primarily uses visual comparisons and convergence metrics (e.g., total variation distance) to validate the theoretical findings.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: While the theoretical guarantees are strong, the analysis depends on several technical assumptions including ℓ2-accurate score estimation and control over the Jacobian estimation errors. Moreover, the convergence bounds have dependencies on the problem dimension and logarithmic factors, which may be sub-optimal in high-dimensional settings. The experimental validation focused mainly on the deterministic sampler, leaving the stochastic variant less explored in practice.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential future work includes refining the theoretical dependency on the dimension, exploring higher-order ODE solvers (such as third-order methods) for further acceleration, and extending the acceleration ideas to SDE-based samplers. There is also scope for investigating practical implementations that combine these accelerated methods with additional training or optimization strategies to further improve sampling speed and fidelity.

{"arxiv_id":"2402.12376v4","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2402.12376v4","title":"FiT: Flexible Vision Transformer for Diffusion Model","authors":["Zeyu Lu","Zidong Wang","Di Huang","Chengyue Wu","Xihui Liu","Wanli Ouyang","Lei Bai"],"published_date":"2024-02-19T18:59:07Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Nature is infinitely resolution-free. In the context of this reality,
existing diffusion models, such as Diffusion Transformers, often face
challenges when processing image resolutions outside of their trained domain.
To overcome this limitation, we present the Flexible Vision Transformer (FiT),
a transformer architecture specifically designed for generating images with
unrestricted resolutions and aspect ratios. Unlike traditional methods that
perceive images as static-resolution grids, FiT conceptualizes images as
sequences of dynamically-sized tokens. This perspective enables a flexible
training strategy that effortlessly adapts to diverse aspect ratios during both
training and inference phases, thus promoting resolution generalization and
eliminating biases induced by image cropping. Enhanced by a meticulously
adjusted network structure and the integration of training-free extrapolation
techniques, FiT exhibits remarkable flexibility in resolution extrapolation
generation. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the exceptional performance
of FiT across a broad range of resolutions, showcasing its effectiveness both
within and beyond its training resolution distribution. Repository available at
https://github.com/whlzy/FiT.","github_url":"https://github.com/whlzy/FiT","main_contributions":"The paper introduces FiT, a Flexible Vision Transformer designed for diffusion models that can generate images of arbitrary resolutions and aspect ratios. It overcomes limitations of prior transformer-based diffusion models (e.g., DiT) by modeling images as sequences of dynamically-sized tokens, preserving the original aspect ratio during training, and enabling resolution extrapolation.","methodology":"FiT employs a flexible training pipeline that avoids cropping and resizing biases by padding sequences to a maximum token length and processing variable-length tokens. The network architecture incorporates modifications over DiT, such as replacing standard Multi-Head Self-Attention with Masked MHSA to handle padding tokens, introducing 2D Rotary Positional Embeddings (2D RoPE) for better resolution generalization, and using SwiGLU in place of the traditional MLP. Furthermore, the paper integrates training-free resolution extrapolation techniques (VisionNTK and VisionYaRN) to adapt positional embeddings for out-of-distribution resolutions.","experimental_setup":"The experiments are conducted using the ImageNet dataset with evaluations performed on both in-distribution (e.g., 256x256, 160x320, 128x384) and out-of-distribution resolutions (e.g., 320x320, 224x448, 160x480). The validation uses metrics such as FID-50K, sFID, Inception Score, Precision, and Recall. Comparisons are made against state-of-the-art models including BigGAN, StyleGAN-XL, MaskGIT, CDM, U-ViT, ADM, LDM, MDT, and DiT.","limitations":"The paper acknowledges that FiT-XL/2, while showing strong extrapolation capabilities, performs slightly worse at the 256x256 resolution compared to DiT-XL/2 when trained for only 1.8M steps. It also notes that the work is limited by computational resources and only explores training-free resolution extrapolation techniques, without investigating alternative methods that might require additional training.","future_research_directions":"Future research could explore training FiT models on higher resolutions with longer token lengths, experimenting with learning-based resolution extrapolation methods, and further refining dynamic positional embedding techniques. Additionally, extending the framework to other generative tasks and benchmarking on broader datasets could provide further insights."}
