Metrics include the l2 norm between the reversed and original trigger, True Positive Rate (TPR), and True Negative Rate (TNR), with comparisons made against a baseline method.","limitations":"While TERD shows high efficacy, some limitations include potential degradation in performance under extremely low trigger scales (adaptive attacks) and the assumption that certain attack parameters conform to the unified formulation. The solution may also have issues when faced with extremely subtle triggers that closely resemble benign noise, and the overall defense relies on access to key model dynamics which may not always be feasible.","future_research_directions":"Future work could extend the framework to other types of generative models beyond diffusion and SDE-based systems, explore more robust detection mechanisms for adaptive backdoor attacks, improve the efficiency of the reverse engineering steps, and study the integration of TERD in real-time, large-scale, and diverse practical deployment scenarios."}
new_method:
Below is the outcome of step 3—a proposal for an entirely new method inspired by both FiT (the Base Method) and the accelerated sampler approach from “Accelerating Convergence of Score-Based Diffusion Models, Provably.”

──────────────────────────────
Proposed Method: Accelerated Flexible Diffusion Transformer (AFiT)

Overview:
AFiT extends FiT’s flexible resolution capabilities by embedding an accelerated sampling engine directly into the diffusion process. Whereas FiT robustly handles arbitrary aspect ratios and resolution extrapolation via dynamic tokenization and training-free positional extrapolation, AFiT targets the inherent bottleneck of slow convergence (especially when generating high-resolution or out-of-distribution images) by integrating a higher‐order ODE-inspired accelerated sampler.

Key Innovations:
1. Dynamic Token Acceleration:
 • Retain FiT’s design of splitting images into dynamically sized tokens with tailored 2D rotary embeddings (RoPE) that support arbitrary resolutions.
 • Introduce an accelerated update module that applies a momentum-inspired corrective term directly at the token level. This module leverages higher-order approximations (as in the accelerated sampler approach) to allow larger and adaptive diffusion steps without sacrificing image fidelity.

2. Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver:
 • Replace the standard DDIM-like integration step (used by FiT) with an adaptive, high-order ODE solver designed for variable-length token sequences.
 • The solver computes token-wise diffusion updates using a second-order correction, thereby accelerating convergence from the conventional O(1/T) rate towards an improved O(1/T²) rate.
 • Integrate error estimation routines that dynamically adjust the step size per token block, addressing the diverse resolution challenges intrinsic to FiT’s flexible representations.

3. Joint Architecture and Sampler Training:
 • Simultaneously train the transformer backbone and the accelerated sampling module end-to-end on a broad mix of resolutions. This coupling ensures that the learned token representations are well-conditioned for both standard and extrapolated resolutions as well as for aggressive, high-step-size updates.
 • Incorporate a secondary loss term that penalizes discrepancies between accelerated sampler predictions and denoised targets, enforcing consistency across varying step sizes and token scales.

Benefits:
• Retains FiT’s resolution flexibility and cropping bias elimination while significantly reducing the number of diffusion timesteps required for high-quality image generation.
• Minimizes computational overhead during inference by converging in fewer iterations, making AFiT attractive for real-time or resource-constrained applications.
• By dynamically adapting step sizes across tokens, the method mitigates issues of uneven convergence when generating images with complex aspect ratios or out-of-distribution resolutions.

Experimental Evaluation:
• Benchmark the approach on datasets such as ImageNet while evaluating standard metrics (FID, Inception Score) and computational efficiency (number of function evaluations, inference time).
• Conduct ablation studies to isolate the contributions of the accelerated sampler and adaptive token-level corrections, comparing against both FiT and conventional accelerated diffusion methods.

Conclusion:
AFiT embodies a comprehensive rethinking of the FiT framework by directly targeting its convergence limitations. By marrying flexible token-based image representation with a principled, high-order sampling acceleration inspired by ODE solvers, AFiT promises to deliver efficient, high-fidelity generation across an unprecedented range of resolutions—a significant leap forward in the state of diffusion models.

──────────────────────────────

This proposal goes well beyond a simple modification of FiT, as it re-imagines the diffusion sampling process for flexible architectures, potentially addressing both computational efficiency and resolution generalization challenges simultaneously.
verification_policy:
Below is a high-level experimental plan that includes three distinct experiments. Each experiment is designed to be realistic and implementable in Python (using libraries such as PyTorch, NumPy, and standard evaluation toolkits) and targets one of the core innovations in AFiT. The goal is to isolate the contributions of the new modules and demonstrate overall superiority compared to the baseline FiT method.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 1: Evaluating Dynamic Token Acceleration

Objective:
• Test the effect of incorporating a momentum-inspired corrective term at the token level.
• Compare image fidelity, convergence speed, and resolution generalization between AFiT’s dynamic token acceleration and the standard FiT token extraction process.

Plan:
• Implementation: Modify the FiT codebase to include an accelerated update module that applies higher‐order corrections on the token representations. This will be implemented as an additional PyTorch module that computes the momentum term before feeding tokens into subsequent transformer blocks.
• Metrics: Use standard evaluation metrics (FID, Inception Score) and track convergence metrics (e.g., improvement in reconstruction error per step) on a benchmark dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10 or a resolution-varied subset of ImageNet).
• Ablation: Run experiments with and without the acceleration module to quantify its impact. This experiment can be scripted in Python, and a simple snippet might look like:
  
  # Pseudocode snippet for dynamic token acceleration module integration
  class TokenAcceleration(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, momentum=0.9):
     super().__init__()
     self.momentum = momentum
     self.linear = nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim)
    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
     # Compute momentum-inspired update
     update = self.linear(token_features) + self.momentum * previous_features
     return update
  
• Validation: Compare accelerated iterations versus standard integration steps in terms of both computational overhead (e.g., inference time, number of function evaluations) and output quality at different resolutions.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 2: Analysis of the Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver

Objective:
• Demonstrate that using an adaptive, high-order (second-order) ODE solver results in faster convergence (e.g., improved O(1/T²)) compared to conventional first-order schemes like DDIM.
• Verify that token-wise adaptive step size adjustments lead to consistent quality across different image resolutions.

Plan:
• Implementation: Replace the standard DDIM-like integration step in the FiT framework with an adaptive second-order solver. Python implementations can leverage numerical libraries (e.g., SciPy’s ODE solvers) or a custom PyTorch integration scheme that computes a second-order correction.
• Metrics: Record the total number of diffusion timesteps required to achieve a fixed quality threshold and measure image quality metrics (FID) as a function of the number of timesteps.
• Ablation: Run both the standard DDIM-like solver and the high-order ODE solver on the same set of images; record differences in convergence rate and error estimation accuracy. A simplified code snippet might be:
  
  def high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update):
    # Compute a second-order term (simplified example)
    correction = compute_derivative(token)
    return token + step_size * correction + (step_size**2) * previous_update
  
• Validation: Plot convergence curves for both solvers. The experimental results should show fewer timesteps required for convergence and a lower aggregate error when using the adaptive high-order ODE solver.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 3: Joint Training of Transformer Backbone and Accelerated Sampler

Objective:
• Assess the benefits of simultaneously training the transformer backbone along with the accelerated sampling module, especially how the secondary loss term improves consistency across diffusion step sizes.
• Determine the trade-off between training complexity and final inference performance.

Plan:
• Implementation: Set up an end-to-end training loop in PyTorch that minimizes a composite loss function. The loss consists of:
  1. A standard diffusion loss (e.g., denoising score matching loss).
  2. A secondary loss term that penalizes discrepancies between accelerated sampler predictions and ground-truth denoised tokens.
• Metrics: Monitor training stability, convergence speed, and final evaluation metrics on a held-out test set using metrics like FID and computation time.
• Ablation: Compare the full joint training method to a baseline that trains the backbone and sampler separately. A simplified loss formulation might be:
  
  loss_total = loss_diffusion + λ * loss_acceleration
  
  where λ is a weight hyperparameter tuned during experiments.
• Validation: Use Python to run grid-search or use libraries like Ray Tune or Optuna for hyperparameter optimization. Demonstrate that joint training consistently yields superior high-resolution generation quality and faster convergence.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion and Integration

After running all experiments:
• Aggregate the metrics to show that AFiT attains high-fidelity generation with fewer diffusion steps and reduced computational overhead.
• Provide visualizations (convergence plots, FID vs. timesteps) and qualitative examples of image outputs across varying resolutions.
• Compose ablation studies in a report to underscore the individual contributions of dynamic token acceleration, the high-order ODE solver, and joint training.

Each of these experiments is built on realistic modifications to existing diffusion frameworks, is implementable in Python, and leverages standard deep learning libraries. This experimental plan should robustly demonstrate the superior performance of the AFiT approach over the base FiT method.
experiment_details:
Below is a detailed experimental plan that directly addresses each of the verification points. We outline three experiments, each isolating one of the main innovations (dynamic token acceleration, the adaptive high‐order ODE solver, and joint training with a secondary loss) while ensuring that, where possible, multiple metrics and sub-components are measured in one integrated experiment. In our proposals, we make extensive use of PyTorch (for network modules and training), NumPy (for numerical operations), and standard evaluation libraries (for computing metrics such as FID and Inception Score). We also leverage existing frameworks for hyperparameter tuning (e.g., Ray Tune or Optuna) to keep implementation realistic and reliable. The design of each experiment is inspired by the experimental design described in the original research, so that our validation closely reflects the original methods with modifications clearly isolating the contributions of AFiT over FiT.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 1: Evaluating Dynamic Token Acceleration

Objective:
• Demonstrate that incorporating a momentum-inspired corrective term at the token level improves image fidelity, accelerates convergence, and enhances resolution generalization.
• Quantify the comparative study between the AFiT dynamic token acceleration and the baseline FiT token extraction.

Plan:
1. Implementation:
   • Begin with the standard FiT framework and insert a new PyTorch module called TokenAcceleration. This module computes a momentum-corrected update on token features before passing them onto subsequent transformer blocks. 
   • The update uses both the current token features and the features from the previous iteration, scaled by a momentum parameter (default value 0.9 but tuneable).
   • This module can be easily inserted into the FiT pipeline via a wrapper function that replaces the vanilla token extraction.

Below is an example PyTorch snippet:

------------------------------------------------------
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class TokenAcceleration(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, momentum=0.9):
        super(TokenAcceleration, self).__init__()
        self.momentum = momentum
        self.linear = nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim)
    
    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        # Compute linear transformation of the token features
        transformed = self.linear(token_features)
        # Combine with momentum-scaled previous features
        update = transformed + self.momentum * previous_features
        return update

# Example integration into a transformer block:

class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim):
        super(TransformerBlock, self).__init__()
        self.token_acceleration = TokenAcceleration(input_dim)
        # Add the rest of the transformer elements
        self.attention = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=input_dim, num_heads=8)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim*4), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(input_dim*4, input_dim))

    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        accelerated_tokens = self.token_acceleration(token_features, previous_features)
        # Self-attention and further MLP computations follow
        attended_tokens, _ = self.attention(accelerated_tokens, accelerated_tokens, accelerated_tokens)
        out = self.mlp(attended_tokens)
        return out

# When training, one must maintain and update the previous_features for each iteration.
------------------------------------------------------

2. Metrics:
   • Evaluate the image quality using FID and Inception Score on benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and/or a resolution-varied subset of ImageNet.
   • Track convergence metrics by logging the reconstruction error or loss per optimization step.
   • Measure computational overhead by analyzing inference time and the number of function evaluations, comparing both the accelerated and the baseline methods.

3. Ablation Study:
   • Conduct a controlled comparison using the same training and validation splits with and without the TokenAcceleration module.
   • Verify if the accelerated tokens show faster convergence (e.g., lower reconstruction error in fewer iterations) and improved generation quality especially when generalizing to varying resolutions.

4. Validation:
   • Plot convergence curves (loss vs steps), FID scores across iteration count, and compare computational overhead.
   • Visualize qualitative samples to confirm that high-resolution details are more faithfully preserved.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 2: Analysis of the Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver

Objective:
• Validate that substituting the conventional first-order (e.g., DDIM-based) integration with an adaptive second-order ODE solver results in faster convergence and overall better error performance.
• Demonstrate that token-wise adaptive step-size adjustment leads to uniform image quality across different resolutions.

Plan:
1. Implementation:
   • Alter the diffusion process integration in the FiT pipeline by replacing the standard DDIM-like step with a high-order ODE solver.
   • The update function will incorporate both a first-order rate term and a second-order correction. This correction can be computed either using PyTorch’s automatic differentiation or by leveraging routines from numerical libraries (like SciPy) where possible.
   • An example update function is provided below:

------------------------------------------------------
def compute_derivative(token):
    # In practice, this would be derived from the gradient of the diffusion loss with respect to the token.
    # Here we provide a placeholder using a simple operation as an example.
    return torch.tanh(token)  # Replace with an appropriate derivative estimate

def high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update):
    # Compute the derivative as the first-order term
    first_order_term = compute_derivative(token)
    # Compute a second-order correction term (this is a simplified version)
    second_order_term = compute_derivative(first_order_term)
    # Adaptive high-order update (assumes availability of previous update to derive correction)
    update = token + step_size * first_order_term + (step_size ** 2) * second_order_term + 0.5 * previous_update
    return update

# Integration into the diffusion loop:
def diffusion_step(token, step_size, previous_update):
    new_token = high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update)
    return new_token
------------------------------------------------------

2. Metrics:
   • Track the number of diffusion timesteps required to achieve a fixed image quality threshold (using FID).
   • Plot FID as a function of the number of timesteps for both the standard and high-order methods.
   • Evaluate consistency by comparing performance across different image resolutions.

3. Ablation Study:
   • Run experiments using both the standard DDIM-like solver and the newly implemented adaptive high-order solver.
   • Log and compare convergence curves and aggregate errors. The expected outcome is that the adaptive solver achieves a comparable or lower aggregate error in substantially fewer timesteps (ideally demonstrating an O(1/T²) improvement).

4. Validation:
   • Create plots (e.g., FID vs. timesteps) to clearly demonstrate fewer timesteps are needed for convergence with the high-order solver.
   • Include a table summarizing the number of function evaluations, overall error estimates, and speed metrics.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 3: Joint Training of Transformer Backbone and Accelerated Sampler

Objective:
• Evaluate the performance gain from simultaneous training of the transformer backbone and the accelerated sampler.
• Determine the trade-off between added training complexity and improvements in final inference performance.
• Assess the effect of a secondary loss term that enforces consistency between the sampler’s token predictions and the ground-truth denoised tokens.

Plan:
1. Implementation:
   • Set up a full end-to-end training loop in PyTorch to jointly optimize the transformer backbone and the dynamic accelerated sampler.
   • Define a composite loss function with two parts:
    (a) The standard diffusion (denoising score matching) loss.
    (b) A secondary loss term (e.g., mean squared error) that penalizes discrepancies between the accelerated predictions and ground-truth tokens.
   • Use a hyperparameter λ to balance these losses.

Below is an example training loop and composite loss definition:

------------------------------------------------------
import torch.optim as optim

# Assume model_backbone and sampler are defined (which include the TokenAcceleration modules as appropriate)
class AFiTModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, sampler):
        super(AFiTModel, self).__init__()
        self.backbone = backbone
        self.sampler = sampler

    def forward(self, x, previous_features):
        # x is the input image, previous_features are carried tokens from the previous step.
        token_features = self.backbone(x)
        # The sampler provides an accelerated update of token features
        tokens_updated = self.sampler(token_features, previous_features)
        return tokens_updated

# Composite loss function
def compute_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens, diffusion_loss_weight=1.0, acceleration_loss_weight=1.0):
    # Standard diffusion loss (e.g., mean squared error for denoising)
    loss_diffusion = ((predicted_tokens - ground_truth_tokens) ** 2).mean()
    # Secondary loss enforcing sample consistency
    loss_acceleration = nn.functional.l1_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens)
    # Total loss with hyperparameter balancing
    loss_total = diffusion_loss_weight * loss_diffusion + acceleration_loss_weight * loss_acceleration
    return loss_total

# Sample training loop
def train_AFiT(model, dataloader, optimizer, device, num_epochs=50, λ=1.0):
    model.to(device)
    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        for batch in dataloader:
            x, ground_truth_tokens = batch['image'].to(device), batch['token_target'].to(device)
            previous_features = torch.zeros_like(ground_truth_tokens)  # Initialization for previous
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            predicted_tokens = model(x, previous_features)
            loss = compute_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens, diffusion_loss_weight=1.0, acceleration_loss_weight=λ)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
        print(f"Epoch [{epoch+1}/{num_epochs}], Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
------------------------------------------------------

2. Metrics:
   • Monitor training curves (loss and other statistics) over epochs.
   • Evaluate inference performance on a held-out test set using FID, as well as computation times.
   • Monitor consistency across different diffusion steps by recording per-step training dynamics.
   • Use grid-search methods (via Ray Tune or Optuna) to optimize the hyperparameter λ and possibly other relevant settings.

3. Ablation Study:
   • Compare the joint training regime with a baseline where the transformer backbone and sampling module are trained separately.
   • Analyze the effect of the secondary loss term by varying λ and observing the change in the final quality and stability.
   • Measure any increase in computational overhead during training and contrast this with improvements in the inference stage.

4. Validation:
   • Plot training loss curves and FID score progression across epochs.
   • Provide visual samples from inference at various training checkpoints.
   • Summarize final evaluation metrics (e.g., FID, inference time) in a comparative table to illustrate the benefits of joint training.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion and Integration

After running these experiments, the following steps will integrate the results:

• Aggregate Results:
  - Compile detailed tables and plots comparing metrics (FID, Inception Score, convergence curves, diffusion steps vs. quality) between AFiT (with dynamic token acceleration, adaptive ODE solver, and joint training) and the baseline FiT implementation.
  - Highlight computational overhead metrics alongside image fidelity metrics.

• Visualizations:
  - Convergence plots: Training loss and reconstruction error curves.
  - FID vs. timesteps graphs to show the efficiency of the high-order solver.
  - Side-by-side qualitative examples of the generated images across varying resolutions.

• Ablation Reports:
  - Prepare detailed ablation studies, each one isolating the contributions of the dynamic token acceleration, the high-order ODE solver, and joint training.
  - Document the improvements achieved by each module, both individually and in combination.

• Robustness Checks:
  - Validate across different dataset splits (e.g., CIFAR-10, resolution-varied ImageNet subsets) to confirm that the improvements generalize.

By following this experimental design, you will have a comprehensive verification plan that robustly demonstrates AFiT’s performance improvements over FiT. The use of well-established deep learning libraries, detailed logging and plotting of convergence metrics, and ablation studies ensure a reliable, reproducible, and high-fidelity evaluation of each innovation.
experiment_code:
Below is an integrated Python implementation that meets the detailed instructions. In this code we implement three experiments that simulate the research design using PyTorch. We generate synthetic data for a quick run‐through (the “test” runs use only one or two iterations so that they finish immediately). Each experiment logs detailed metrics via print statements and saves results as PDF figures using matplotlib. The filenames follow the required naming format. 

Below the code we also list the required Python libraries.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Required libraries:
  • torch
  • torchvision
  • numpy
  • matplotlib
  • seaborn (optional for aesthetics, though here we use matplotlib mainly)
  • (Optionally: ray[tune] or optuna could be added for hyperparameter tuning but are not used in this minimal example)
  
To install required packages, you might use:
  pip install torch torchvision numpy matplotlib seaborn
"""

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import time

###############################
# Experiment 1: Evaluating Dynamic Token Acceleration
###############################

class TokenAcceleration(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, momentum=0.9):
        super(TokenAcceleration, self).__init__()
        self.momentum = momentum
        self.linear = nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim)
    
    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        # Compute linear transformation of the token features
        transformed = self.linear(token_features)
        # Combine with momentum-scaled previous features
        update = transformed + self.momentum * previous_features
        return update

class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim):
        super(TransformerBlock, self).__init__()
        self.token_acceleration = TokenAcceleration(input_dim)
        # Self-attention layer.
        self.attention = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=input_dim, num_heads=8)
        # MLP layers
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim * 4),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(input_dim * 4, input_dim)
        )

    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        # Apply token acceleration module
        accelerated_tokens = self.token_acceleration(token_features, previous_features)
        # PyTorch multihead attention expects (L, N, E); we assume batch-first format and then transpose.
        # For simplicity, we assume token_features shape: (seq_length, batch, feature_dim)
        attended_tokens, _ = self.attention(accelerated_tokens, accelerated_tokens, accelerated_tokens)
        out = self.mlp(attended_tokens)
        return out

def experiment1():
    print("Beginning Experiment 1: Dynamic Token Acceleration")
    
    # Settings
    seq_length = 10      # number of tokens
    batch_size = 2
    feature_dim = 32
    num_iterations = 5   # For demonstration, run only a few iterations (= training steps)
    
    # Initialize synthetic token features and previous features
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    token_features = torch.randn(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    previous_features = torch.zeros_like(token_features)  # initialize previous features to zero
    
    # Initialize transformer block (which includes the TokenAcceleration module)
    transformer = TransformerBlock(feature_dim)
    transformer.train()

    # Create synthetic "ground truth" tokens for loss computation (dummy target)
    ground_truth = torch.randn(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    
    # Optimizer (for demonstration only)
    optimizer = optim.Adam(transformer.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    
    loss_history = []
    for iteration in range(num_iterations):
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        # forward pass: get updated token features
        output_tokens = transformer(token_features, previous_features)
        # update previous_features with the current token_features (simulate momentum update)
        previous_features = token_features.clone()
        # dummy loss: mean squared error between output tokens and ground truth
        loss = ((output_tokens - ground_truth) ** 2).mean()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        
        loss_val = loss.item()
        loss_history.append(loss_val)
        print(f"Iteration {iteration+1}/{num_iterations}, Loss: {loss_val:.4f}")

    # Plot convergence curve: loss vs iterations.
    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
    plt.plot(range(1, num_iterations+1), loss_history, marker='o', linestyle='-', color='blue')
    plt.xlabel("Iteration")
    plt.ylabel("Loss")
    plt.title("Convergence Curve for Dynamic Token Acceleration")
    # Save figure using the required format (topic_condition_size)
    plt.savefig("training_loss_dynamicToken_small.pdf")
    plt.close()
    print("Experiment 1 complete. Saved plot as 'training_loss_dynamicToken_small.pdf'.\n")


###############################
# Experiment 2: Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver
###############################

def compute_derivative(token):
    # In practice, the derivative would come from the gradient of diffusion loss. Here we use a placeholder.
    return torch.tanh(token)

def high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update):
    # First-order term.
    first_order_term = compute_derivative(token)
    # Second-order correction term.
    second_order_term = compute_derivative(first_order_term)
    # Adaptive high-order update equation; a simplified version.
    update = token + step_size * first_order_term + (step_size ** 2) * second_order_term + 0.5 * previous_update
    return update

def diffusion_step(token, step_size, previous_update):
    # Compute a new token via the high-order update.
    new_token = high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update)
    return new_token

def experiment2():
    print("Beginning Experiment 2: Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver")
    
    # Settings for diffusion simulation
    seq_length = 10  # number of tokens in the diffusion process simulation
    batch_size = 2
    feature_dim = 32
    num_steps = 10  # For demonstration, use 10 steps per simulation.
    step_size = 0.1
    
    # Generate a starting token (synthetic noise image representation)
    torch.manual_seed(1)
    token_standard = torch.randn(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    token_highorder = token_standard.clone()  # we simulate both approaches on identical starting noise.
    
    # For the high-order solver, we maintain a previous update
    previous_update = torch.zeros_like(token_highorder)
    
    # Lists to record a dummy "FID score" across diffusion steps
    fid_standard = []
    fid_highorder = []
    
    # For comparison, perform a standard first-order update:
    def standard_diffusion_step(token, step_size):
        # Standard update: token + step_size * derivative
        return token + step_size * compute_derivative(token)
    
    # Simulate diffusion process for a number of steps
    for step in range(1, num_steps+1):
        # Standard update
        token_standard = standard_diffusion_step(token_standard, step_size)
        # For high-order update
        token_highorder = diffusion_step(token_highorder, step_size, previous_update)
        previous_update = compute_derivative(token_highorder)  # update previous derivative for next iteration
        
        # For demonstration, we simulate FID as inversely proportional to step number and noise norm.
        fid_std = 100.0 / step + torch.mean(torch.abs(token_standard)).item()
        fid_ho = 100.0 / step + torch.mean(torch.abs(token_highorder)).item()
        fid_standard.append(fid_std)
        fid_highorder.append(fid_ho)
        print(f"Step {step}/{num_steps}: Standard FID={fid_std:.4f}, High-Order FID={fid_ho:.4f}")
        
    # Plot FID vs steps for both methods.
    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
    plt.plot(range(1, num_steps+1), fid_standard, marker='s', linestyle='--', color='red', label="Standard")
    plt.plot(range(1, num_steps+1), fid_highorder, marker='o', linestyle='-', color='green', label="High-Order")
    plt.xlabel("Diffusion Steps")
    plt.ylabel("Simulated FID")
    plt.title("FID vs. Diffusion Steps: Standard vs. Adaptive High-Order")
    plt.legend()
    plt.savefig("fid_vs_timesteps_highOrder_small.pdf")
    plt.close()
    print("Experiment 2 complete. Saved plot as 'fid_vs_timesteps_highOrder_small.pdf'.\n")


###############################
# Experiment 3: Joint Training of Transformer Backbone and Accelerated Sampler
###############################

# Define a dummy backbone: here we use a simple convolutional network that extracts features.
class DummyBackbone(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels=3, feature_dim=32):
        super(DummyBackbone, self).__init__()
        self.conv = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(in_channels, 16, kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv2d(16, feature_dim, kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1),
            nn.ReLU()
        )
        # We'll flatten spatial dimension into a token sequence.
    
    def forward(self, x):
        out = self.conv(x)  # shape: (batch, feature_dim, H, W)
        batch_size, feature_dim, H, W = out.shape
        # Flatten spatially: (seq_length, batch, feature_dim) where seq_length = H*W
        tokens = out.view(batch_size, feature_dim, -1).permute(2, 0, 1)
        return tokens

# Sampler module that uses TokenAcceleration. (For simplicity, we use the same as in Experiment 1)
class AccelerationSampler(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, feature_dim, momentum=0.9):
        super(AccelerationSampler, self).__init__()
        self.token_acceleration = TokenAcceleration(feature_dim, momentum)
    
    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        return self.token_acceleration(token_features, previous_features)

# Composite AFiT model integrating backbone and sampler
class AFiTModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, sampler):
        super(AFiTModel, self).__init__()
        self.backbone = backbone
        self.sampler = sampler

    def forward(self, x, previous_features):
        token_features = self.backbone(x)
        tokens_updated = self.sampler(token_features, previous_features)
        return tokens_updated

def compute_composite_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens, diffusion_loss_weight=1.0, acceleration_loss_weight=1.0):
    # Standard diffusion (denoising) loss: mean squared error
    loss_diffusion = ((predicted_tokens - ground_truth_tokens) ** 2).mean()
    # Secondary loss enforcing consistency (using L1 loss)
    loss_acceleration = nn.functional.l1_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens)
    # Total loss with balancing lambda (acceleration_loss_weight here)
    loss_total = diffusion_loss_weight * loss_diffusion + acceleration_loss_weight * loss_acceleration
    return loss_total

def experiment3():
    print("Beginning Experiment 3: Joint Training of Transformer Backbone and Accelerated Sampler")
    
    # Settings
    batch_size = 2
    image_size = 16  # Use a small image size for demonstration (16x16)
    in_channels = 3
    feature_dim = 32
    num_epochs = 3  # For a quick run
    lambda_acc = 1.0  # weight for secondary loss
    
    # Create dummy dataset: random images and random token targets
    # Input images: shape (batch, in_channels, H, W)
    torch.manual_seed(2)
    images = torch.randn(batch_size, in_channels, image_size, image_size)
    # Ground truth tokens: obtained from backbone ideally, but here we just use random targets
    # For backbone the token shape will be (seq_length, batch, feature_dim)
    seq_length = image_size * image_size  # because of flattening
    ground_truth_tokens = torch.randn(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    
    # Initialize model components
    backbone = DummyBackbone(in_channels, feature_dim)
    sampler = AccelerationSampler(feature_dim, momentum=0.9)
    model = AFiTModel(backbone, sampler)
    model.train()
    
    optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    
    loss_history = []
    # For simplicity, we initialize previous_features as zeros with same shape as tokens.
    previous_features = torch.zeros(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    
    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        predicted_tokens = model(images, previous_features)
        loss = compute_composite_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens, diffusion_loss_weight=1.0, acceleration_loss_weight=lambda_acc)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        
        loss_val = loss.item()
        loss_history.append(loss_val)
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}/{num_epochs}, Composite Loss: {loss_val:.4f}")
    
    # Plot training loss versus epochs.
    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
    plt.plot(range(1, num_epochs+1), loss_history, marker='o', linestyle='-', color='purple')
    plt.xlabel("Epoch")
    plt.ylabel("Composite Loss")
    plt.title("Joint Training Loss Curve")
    # Save as PDF with naming as required.
    plt.savefig("training_loss_joint_full.pdf")
    plt.close()
    print("Experiment 3 complete. Saved plot as 'training_loss_joint_full.pdf'.\n")


###############################
# Test function to verify the code runs correctly
###############################
def test():
    print("Running tests for all experiments...\n")
    
    start_time = time.time()
    # Run Experiment 1: quick run to test token acceleration module
    experiment1()
    # Run Experiment 2: quick run to test adaptive ODE solver
    experiment2()
    # Run Experiment 3: quick run to test joint training of backbone and sampler
    experiment3()
    
    end_time = time.time()
    print(f"All tests completed in {end_time - start_time:.2f} seconds.")
    
if __name__ == '__main__':
    test()

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Notes:
• The code above uses synthetic data and very few iterations/epochs for testing.
• In practice, you would use real datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, resolution-varied ImageNet) and run much longer training loops.
• All plots are saved explicitly as .pdf files with filenames that meet the naming convention.
• The test() function ensures that the code executes immediately and provides detailed standard output logs.
  
Happy experimenting!

    new_method: Below is the outcome of step 3—a proposal for an entirely new method inspired by both FiT (the Base Method) and the accelerated sampler approach from “Accelerating Convergence of Score-Based Diffusion Models, Provably.”

──────────────────────────────
Proposed Method: Accelerated Flexible Diffusion Transformer (AFiT)

Overview:
AFiT extends FiT’s flexible resolution capabilities by embedding an accelerated sampling engine directly into the diffusion process. Whereas FiT robustly handles arbitrary aspect ratios and resolution extrapolation via dynamic tokenization and training-free positional extrapolation, AFiT targets the inherent bottleneck of slow convergence (especially when generating high-resolution or out-of-distribution images) by integrating a higher‐order ODE-inspired accelerated sampler.

Key Innovations:
1. Dynamic Token Acceleration:
 • Retain FiT’s design of splitting images into dynamically sized tokens with tailored 2D rotary embeddings (RoPE) that support arbitrary resolutions.
 • Introduce an accelerated update module that applies a momentum-inspired corrective term directly at the token level. This module leverages higher-order approximations (as in the accelerated sampler approach) to allow larger and adaptive diffusion steps without sacrificing image fidelity.

2. Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver:
 • Replace the standard DDIM-like integration step (used by FiT) with an adaptive, high-order ODE solver designed for variable-length token sequences.
 • The solver computes token-wise diffusion updates using a second-order correction, thereby accelerating convergence from the conventional O(1/T) rate towards an improved O(1/T²) rate.
 • Integrate error estimation routines that dynamically adjust the step size per token block, addressing the diverse resolution challenges intrinsic to FiT’s flexible representations.

3. Joint Architecture and Sampler Training:
 • Simultaneously train the transformer backbone and the accelerated sampling module end-to-end on a broad mix of resolutions. This coupling ensures that the learned token representations are well-conditioned for both standard and extrapolated resolutions as well as for aggressive, high-step-size updates.
 • Incorporate a secondary loss term that penalizes discrepancies between accelerated sampler predictions and denoised targets, enforcing consistency across varying step sizes and token scales.

Benefits:
• Retains FiT’s resolution flexibility and cropping bias elimination while significantly reducing the number of diffusion timesteps required for high-quality image generation.
• Minimizes computational overhead during inference by converging in fewer iterations, making AFiT attractive for real-time or resource-constrained applications.
• By dynamically adapting step sizes across tokens, the method mitigates issues of uneven convergence when generating images with complex aspect ratios or out-of-distribution resolutions.

Experimental Evaluation:
• Benchmark the approach on datasets such as ImageNet while evaluating standard metrics (FID, Inception Score) and computational efficiency (number of function evaluations, inference time).
• Conduct ablation studies to isolate the contributions of the accelerated sampler and adaptive token-level corrections, comparing against both FiT and conventional accelerated diffusion methods.

Conclusion:
AFiT embodies a comprehensive rethinking of the FiT framework by directly targeting its convergence limitations. By marrying flexible token-based image representation with a principled, high-order sampling acceleration inspired by ODE solvers, AFiT promises to deliver efficient, high-fidelity generation across an unprecedented range of resolutions—a significant leap forward in the state of diffusion models.

──────────────────────────────

This proposal goes well beyond a simple modification of FiT, as it re-imagines the diffusion sampling process for flexible architectures, potentially addressing both computational efficiency and resolution generalization challenges simultaneously.

Each experiment is designed to be realistic and implementable in Python (using libraries such as PyTorch, NumPy, and standard evaluation toolkits) and targets one of the core innovations in AFiT. The goal is to isolate the contributions of the new modules and demonstrate overall superiority compared to the baseline FiT method.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 1: Evaluating Dynamic Token Acceleration

Objective:
• Test the effect of incorporating a momentum-inspired corrective term at the token level.
• Compare image fidelity, convergence speed, and resolution generalization between AFiT’s dynamic token acceleration and the standard FiT token extraction process.

Plan:
• Implementation: Modify the FiT codebase to include an accelerated update module that applies higher‐order corrections on the token representations. This will be implemented as an additional PyTorch module that computes the momentum term before feeding tokens into subsequent transformer blocks.
• Metrics: Use standard evaluation metrics (FID, Inception Score) and track convergence metrics (e.g., improvement in reconstruction error per step) on a benchmark dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10 or a resolution-varied subset of ImageNet).
• Ablation: Run experiments with and without the acceleration module to quantify its impact. This experiment can be scripted in Python, and a simple snippet might look like:
  
  # Pseudocode snippet for dynamic token acceleration module integration

• Validation: Compare accelerated iterations versus standard integration steps in terms of both computational overhead (e.g., inference time, number of function evaluations) and output quality at different resolutions.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 2: Analysis of the Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver

Objective:
• Demonstrate that using an adaptive, high-order (second-order) ODE solver results in faster convergence (e.g., improved O(1/T²)) compared to conventional first-order schemes like DDIM.
• Verify that token-wise adaptive step size adjustments lead to consistent quality across different image resolutions.

Plan:
• Implementation: Replace the standard DDIM-like integration step in the FiT framework with an adaptive second-order solver. Python implementations can leverage numerical libraries (e.g., SciPy’s ODE solvers) or a custom PyTorch integration scheme that computes a second-order correction.
• Metrics: Record the total number of diffusion timesteps required to achieve a fixed quality threshold and measure image quality metrics (FID) as a function of the number of timesteps.
• Ablation: Run both the standard DDIM-like solver and the high-order ODE solver on the same set of images; record differences in convergence rate and error estimation accuracy. A simplified code snippet might be:
  
  def high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update):
    # Compute a second-order term (simplified example)
    correction = compute_derivative(token)
    return token + step_size * correction + (step_size**2) * previous_update
  
• Validation: Plot convergence curves for both solvers. The experimental results should show fewer timesteps required for convergence and a lower aggregate error when using the adaptive high-order ODE solver.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 3: Joint Training of Transformer Backbone and Accelerated Sampler

Objective:
• Assess the benefits of simultaneously training the transformer backbone along with the accelerated sampling module, especially how the secondary loss term improves consistency across diffusion step sizes.
• Determine the trade-off between training complexity and final inference performance.

Plan:
• Implementation: Set up an end-to-end training loop in PyTorch that minimizes a composite loss function. The loss consists of:
  1. A standard diffusion loss (e.g., denoising score matching loss).
  2. A secondary loss term that penalizes discrepancies between accelerated sampler predictions and ground-truth denoised tokens.
• Metrics: Monitor training stability, convergence speed, and final evaluation metrics on a held-out test set using metrics like FID and computation time.
• Ablation: Compare the full joint training method to a baseline that trains the backbone and sampler separately. A simplified loss formulation might be:
  
  loss_total = loss_diffusion + λ * loss_acceleration
  
  where λ is a weight hyperparameter tuned during experiments.
• Validation: Use Python to run grid-search or use libraries like Ray Tune or Optuna for hyperparameter optimization. Demonstrate that joint training consistently yields superior high-resolution generation quality and faster convergence.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion and Integration

After running all experiments:
• Aggregate the metrics to show that AFiT attains high-fidelity generation with fewer diffusion steps and reduced computational overhead.
• Provide visualizations (convergence plots, FID vs. timesteps) and qualitative examples of image outputs across varying resolutions.
• Compose ablation studies in a report to underscore the individual contributions of dynamic token acceleration, the high-order ODE solver, and joint training.

Each of these experiments is built on realistic modifications to existing diffusion frameworks, is implementable in Python, and leverages standard deep learning libraries. This experimental plan should robustly demonstrate the superior performance of the AFiT approach over the base FiT method.
    
    experiment_details: Below is a detailed experimental plan that directly addresses each of the verification points. We outline three experiments, each isolating one of the main innovations (dynamic token acceleration, the adaptive high‐order ODE solver, and joint training with a secondary loss) while ensuring that, where possible, multiple metrics and sub-components are measured in one integrated experiment. In our proposals, we make extensive use of PyTorch (for network modules and training), NumPy (for numerical operations), and standard evaluation libraries (for computing metrics such as FID and Inception Score). We also leverage existing frameworks for hyperparameter tuning (e.g., Ray Tune or Optuna) to keep implementation realistic and reliable. The design of each experiment is inspired by the experimental design described in the original research, so that our validation closely reflects the original methods with modifications clearly isolating the contributions of AFiT over FiT.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 1: Evaluating Dynamic Token Acceleration

Objective:
• Demonstrate that incorporating a momentum-inspired corrective term at the token level improves image fidelity, accelerates convergence, and enhances resolution generalization.
• Quantify the comparative study between the AFiT dynamic token acceleration and the baseline FiT token extraction.

Plan:
1. Implementation:
   • Begin with the standard FiT framework and insert a new PyTorch module called TokenAcceleration. This module computes a momentum-corrected update on token features before passing them onto subsequent transformer blocks. 
   • The update uses both the current token features and the features from the previous iteration, scaled by a momentum parameter (default value 0.9 but tuneable).
   • This module can be easily inserted into the FiT pipeline via a wrapper function that replaces the vanilla token extraction.

Below is an example PyTorch snippet:

------------------------------------------------------
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class TokenAcceleration(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, momentum=0.9):
        super(TokenAcceleration, self).__init__()
        self.momentum = momentum
        self.linear = nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim)
    
    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        # Compute linear transformation of the token features
        transformed = self.linear(token_features)
        # Combine with momentum-scaled previous features
        update = transformed + self.momentum * previous_features
        return update

# Example integration into a transformer block:

class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim):
        super(TransformerBlock, self).__init__()
        self.token_acceleration = TokenAcceleration(input_dim)
        # Add the rest of the transformer elements
        self.attention = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=input_dim, num_heads=8)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim*4), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(input_dim*4, input_dim))

    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        accelerated_tokens = self.token_acceleration(token_features, previous_features)
        # Self-attention and further MLP computations follow
        attended_tokens, _ = self.attention(accelerated_tokens, accelerated_tokens, accelerated_tokens)
        out = self.mlp(attended_tokens)
        return out

# When training, one must maintain and update the previous_features for each iteration.
------------------------------------------------------

2. Metrics:
   • Evaluate the image quality using FID and Inception Score on benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and/or a resolution-varied subset of ImageNet.
   • Track convergence metrics by logging the reconstruction error or loss per optimization step.
   • Measure computational overhead by analyzing inference time and the number of function evaluations, comparing both the accelerated and the baseline methods.

3. Ablation Study:
   • Conduct a controlled comparison using the same training and validation splits with and without the TokenAcceleration module.
   • Verify if the accelerated tokens show faster convergence (e.g., lower reconstruction error in fewer iterations) and improved generation quality especially when generalizing to varying resolutions.

4. Validation:
   • Plot convergence curves (loss vs steps), FID scores across iteration count, and compare computational overhead.
   • Visualize qualitative samples to confirm that high-resolution details are more faithfully preserved.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 2: Analysis of the Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver

Objective:
• Validate that substituting the conventional first-order (e.g., DDIM-based) integration with an adaptive second-order ODE solver results in faster convergence and overall better error performance.
• Demonstrate that token-wise adaptive step-size adjustment leads to uniform image quality across different resolutions.

Plan:
1. Implementation:
   • Alter the diffusion process integration in the FiT pipeline by replacing the standard DDIM-like step with a high-order ODE solver.
   • The update function will incorporate both a first-order rate term and a second-order correction. This correction can be computed either using PyTorch’s automatic differentiation or by leveraging routines from numerical libraries (like SciPy) where possible.
   • An example update function is provided below:

------------------------------------------------------
def compute_derivative(token):
    # In practice, this would be derived from the gradient of the diffusion loss with respect to the token.
    # Here we provide a placeholder using a simple operation as an example.
    return torch.tanh(token)  # Replace with an appropriate derivative estimate

def high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update):
    # Compute the derivative as the first-order term
    first_order_term = compute_derivative(token)
    # Compute a second-order correction term (this is a simplified version)
    second_order_term = compute_derivative(first_order_term)
    # Adaptive high-order update (assumes availability of previous update to derive correction)
    update = token + step_size * first_order_term + (step_size ** 2) * second_order_term + 0.5 * previous_update
    return update

# Integration into the diffusion loop:
def diffusion_step(token, step_size, previous_update):
    new_token = high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update)
    return new_token
------------------------------------------------------

2. Metrics:
   • Track the number of diffusion timesteps required to achieve a fixed image quality threshold (using FID).
   • Plot FID as a function of the number of timesteps for both the standard and high-order methods.
   • Evaluate consistency by comparing performance across different image resolutions.

3. Ablation Study:
   • Run experiments using both the standard DDIM-like solver and the newly implemented adaptive high-order solver.
   • Log and compare convergence curves and aggregate errors. The expected outcome is that the adaptive solver achieves a comparable or lower aggregate error in substantially fewer timesteps (ideally demonstrating an O(1/T²) improvement).

4. Validation:
   • Create plots (e.g., FID vs. timesteps) to clearly demonstrate fewer timesteps are needed for convergence with the high-order solver.
   • Include a table summarizing the number of function evaluations, overall error estimates, and speed metrics.

──────────────────────────────
Experiment 3: Joint Training of Transformer Backbone and Accelerated Sampler

Objective:
• Evaluate the performance gain from simultaneous training of the transformer backbone and the accelerated sampler.
• Determine the trade-off between added training complexity and improvements in final inference performance.
• Assess the effect of a secondary loss term that enforces consistency between the sampler’s token predictions and the ground-truth denoised tokens.

Plan:
1. Implementation:
   • Set up a full end-to-end training loop in PyTorch to jointly optimize the transformer backbone and the dynamic accelerated sampler.
   • Define a composite loss function with two parts:
    (a) The standard diffusion (denoising score matching) loss.
    (b) A secondary loss term (e.g., mean squared error) that penalizes discrepancies between the accelerated predictions and ground-truth tokens.
   • Use a hyperparameter λ to balance these losses.

Below is an example training loop and composite loss definition:

------------------------------------------------------
import torch.optim as optim

# Assume model_backbone and sampler are defined (which include the TokenAcceleration modules as appropriate)
class AFiTModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, sampler):
        super(AFiTModel, self).__init__()
        self.backbone = backbone
        self.sampler = sampler

    def forward(self, x, previous_features):
        # x is the input image, previous_features are carried tokens from the previous step.
        token_features = self.backbone(x)
        # The sampler provides an accelerated update of token features
        tokens_updated = self.sampler(token_features, previous_features)
        return tokens_updated

# Composite loss function
def compute_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens, diffusion_loss_weight=1.0, acceleration_loss_weight=1.0):
    # Standard diffusion loss (e.g., mean squared error for denoising)
    loss_diffusion = ((predicted_tokens - ground_truth_tokens) ** 2).mean()
    # Secondary loss enforcing sample consistency
    loss_acceleration = nn.functional.l1_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens)
    # Total loss with hyperparameter balancing
    loss_total = diffusion_loss_weight * loss_diffusion + acceleration_loss_weight * loss_acceleration
    return loss_total

# Sample training loop
def train_AFiT(model, dataloader, optimizer, device, num_epochs=50, λ=1.0):
    model.to(device)
    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        for batch in dataloader:
            x, ground_truth_tokens = batch['image'].to(device), batch['token_target'].to(device)
            previous_features = torch.zeros_like(ground_truth_tokens)  # Initialization for previous
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            predicted_tokens = model(x, previous_features)
            loss = compute_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens, diffusion_loss_weight=1.0, acceleration_loss_weight=λ)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
        print(f"Epoch [{epoch+1}/{num_epochs}], Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
------------------------------------------------------

2. Metrics:
   • Monitor training curves (loss and other statistics) over epochs.
   • Evaluate inference performance on a held-out test set using FID, as well as computation times.
   • Monitor consistency across different diffusion steps by recording per-step training dynamics.
   • Use grid-search methods (via Ray Tune or Optuna) to optimize the hyperparameter λ and possibly other relevant settings.

3. Ablation Study:
   • Compare the joint training regime with a baseline where the transformer backbone and sampling module are trained separately.
   • Analyze the effect of the secondary loss term by varying λ and observing the change in the final quality and stability.
   • Measure any increase in computational overhead during training and contrast this with improvements in the inference stage.

4. Validation:
   • Plot training loss curves and FID score progression across epochs.
   • Provide visual samples from inference at various training checkpoints.
   • Summarize final evaluation metrics (e.g., FID, inference time) in a comparative table to illustrate the benefits of joint training.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion and Integration

After running these experiments, the following steps will integrate the results:

• Aggregate Results:
  - Compile detailed tables and plots comparing metrics (FID, Inception Score, convergence curves, diffusion steps vs. quality) between AFiT (with dynamic token acceleration, adaptive ODE solver, and joint training) and the baseline FiT implementation.
  - Highlight computational overhead metrics alongside image fidelity metrics.

• Visualizations:
  - Convergence plots: Training loss and reconstruction error curves.
  - FID vs. timesteps graphs to show the efficiency of the high-order solver.
  - Side-by-side qualitative examples of the generated images across varying resolutions.

• Ablation Reports:
  - Prepare detailed ablation studies, each one isolating the contributions of the dynamic token acceleration, the high-order ODE solver, and joint training.
  - Document the improvements achieved by each module, both individually and in combination.

• Robustness Checks:
  - Validate across different dataset splits (e.g., CIFAR-10, resolution-varied ImageNet subsets) to confirm that the improvements generalize.

By following this experimental design, you will have a comprehensive verification plan that robustly demonstrates AFiT’s performance improvements over FiT. The use of well-established deep learning libraries, detailed logging and plotting of convergence metrics, and ablation studies ensure a reliable, reproducible, and high-fidelity evaluation of each innovation.
    
    
    # Codes
    
    experiment_code: Below is an integrated Python implementation that meets the detailed instructions. In this code we implement three experiments that simulate the research design using PyTorch. We generate synthetic data for a quick run‐through (the “test” runs use only one or two iterations so that they finish immediately). Each experiment logs detailed metrics via print statements and saves results as PDF figures using matplotlib. The filenames follow the required naming format. 

Below the code we also list the required Python libraries.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Required libraries:
  • torch
  • torchvision
  • numpy
  • matplotlib
  • seaborn (optional for aesthetics, though here we use matplotlib mainly)
  • (Optionally: ray[tune] or optuna could be added for hyperparameter tuning but are not used in this minimal example)
  
To install required packages, you might use:
  pip install torch torchvision numpy matplotlib seaborn
"""

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import time

###############################
# Experiment 1: Evaluating Dynamic Token Acceleration
###############################

class TokenAcceleration(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, momentum=0.9):
        super(TokenAcceleration, self).__init__()
        self.momentum = momentum
        self.linear = nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim)
    
    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        # Compute linear transformation of the token features
        transformed = self.linear(token_features)
        # Combine with momentum-scaled previous features
        update = transformed + self.momentum * previous_features
        return update

class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim):
        super(TransformerBlock, self).__init__()
        self.token_acceleration = TokenAcceleration(input_dim)
        # Self-attention layer.
        self.attention = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=input_dim, num_heads=8)
        # MLP layers
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, input_dim * 4),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(input_dim * 4, input_dim)
        )

    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        # Apply token acceleration module
        accelerated_tokens = self.token_acceleration(token_features, previous_features)
        # PyTorch multihead attention expects (L, N, E); we assume batch-first format and then transpose.
        # For simplicity, we assume token_features shape: (seq_length, batch, feature_dim)
        attended_tokens, _ = self.attention(accelerated_tokens, accelerated_tokens, accelerated_tokens)
        out = self.mlp(attended_tokens)
        return out

def experiment1():
    print("Beginning Experiment 1: Dynamic Token Acceleration")
    
    # Settings
    seq_length = 10      # number of tokens
    batch_size = 2
    feature_dim = 32
    num_iterations = 5   # For demonstration, run only a few iterations (= training steps)
    
    # Initialize synthetic token features and previous features
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    token_features = torch.randn(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    previous_features = torch.zeros_like(token_features)  # initialize previous features to zero
    
    # Initialize transformer block (which includes the TokenAcceleration module)
    transformer = TransformerBlock(feature_dim)
    transformer.train()

    # Create synthetic "ground truth" tokens for loss computation (dummy target)
    ground_truth = torch.randn(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    
    # Optimizer (for demonstration only)
    optimizer = optim.Adam(transformer.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    
    loss_history = []
    for iteration in range(num_iterations):
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        # forward pass: get updated token features
        output_tokens = transformer(token_features, previous_features)
        # update previous_features with the current token_features (simulate momentum update)
        previous_features = token_features.clone()
        # dummy loss: mean squared error between output tokens and ground truth
        loss = ((output_tokens - ground_truth) ** 2).mean()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        
        loss_val = loss.item()
        loss_history.append(loss_val)
        print(f"Iteration {iteration+1}/{num_iterations}, Loss: {loss_val:.4f}")

    # Plot convergence curve: loss vs iterations.
    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
    plt.plot(range(1, num_iterations+1), loss_history, marker='o', linestyle='-', color='blue')
    plt.xlabel("Iteration")
    plt.ylabel("Loss")
    plt.title("Convergence Curve for Dynamic Token Acceleration")
    # Save figure using the required format (topic_condition_size)
    plt.savefig("training_loss_dynamicToken_small.pdf")
    plt.close()
    print("Experiment 1 complete. Saved plot as 'training_loss_dynamicToken_small.pdf'.\n")


###############################
# Experiment 2: Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver
###############################

def compute_derivative(token):
    # In practice, the derivative would come from the gradient of diffusion loss. Here we use a placeholder.
    return torch.tanh(token)

def high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update):
    # First-order term.
    first_order_term = compute_derivative(token)
    # Second-order correction term.
    second_order_term = compute_derivative(first_order_term)
    # Adaptive high-order update equation; a simplified version.
    update = token + step_size * first_order_term + (step_size ** 2) * second_order_term + 0.5 * previous_update
    return update

def diffusion_step(token, step_size, previous_update):
    # Compute a new token via the high-order update.
    new_token = high_order_update(token, step_size, previous_update)
    return new_token

def experiment2():
    print("Beginning Experiment 2: Adaptive High-Order ODE Solver")
    
    # Settings for diffusion simulation
    seq_length = 10  # number of tokens in the diffusion process simulation
    batch_size = 2
    feature_dim = 32
    num_steps = 10  # For demonstration, use 10 steps per simulation.
    step_size = 0.1
    
    # Generate a starting token (synthetic noise image representation)
    torch.manual_seed(1)
    token_standard = torch.randn(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    token_highorder = token_standard.clone()  # we simulate both approaches on identical starting noise.
    
    # For the high-order solver, we maintain a previous update
    previous_update = torch.zeros_like(token_highorder)
    
    # Lists to record a dummy "FID score" across diffusion steps
    fid_standard = []
    fid_highorder = []
    
    # For comparison, perform a standard first-order update:
    def standard_diffusion_step(token, step_size):
        # Standard update: token + step_size * derivative
        return token + step_size * compute_derivative(token)
    
    # Simulate diffusion process for a number of steps
    for step in range(1, num_steps+1):
        # Standard update
        token_standard = standard_diffusion_step(token_standard, step_size)
        # For high-order update
        token_highorder = diffusion_step(token_highorder, step_size, previous_update)
        previous_update = compute_derivative(token_highorder)  # update previous derivative for next iteration
        
        # For demonstration, we simulate FID as inversely proportional to step number and noise norm.
        fid_std = 100.0 / step + torch.mean(torch.abs(token_standard)).item()
        fid_ho = 100.0 / step + torch.mean(torch.abs(token_highorder)).item()
        fid_standard.append(fid_std)
        fid_highorder.append(fid_ho)
        print(f"Step {step}/{num_steps}: Standard FID={fid_std:.4f}, High-Order FID={fid_ho:.4f}")
        
    # Plot FID vs steps for both methods.
    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
    plt.plot(range(1, num_steps+1), fid_standard, marker='s', linestyle='--', color='red', label="Standard")
    plt.plot(range(1, num_steps+1), fid_highorder, marker='o', linestyle='-', color='green', label="High-Order")
    plt.xlabel("Diffusion Steps")
    plt.ylabel("Simulated FID")
    plt.title("FID vs. Diffusion Steps: Standard vs. Adaptive High-Order")
    plt.legend()
    plt.savefig("fid_vs_timesteps_highOrder_small.pdf")
    plt.close()
    print("Experiment 2 complete. Saved plot as 'fid_vs_timesteps_highOrder_small.pdf'.\n")


###############################
# Experiment 3: Joint Training of Transformer Backbone and Accelerated Sampler
###############################

# Define a dummy backbone: here we use a simple convolutional network that extracts features.
class DummyBackbone(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels=3, feature_dim=32):
        super(DummyBackbone, self).__init__()
        self.conv = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(in_channels, 16, kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv2d(16, feature_dim, kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1),
            nn.ReLU()
        )
        # We'll flatten spatial dimension into a token sequence.
    
    def forward(self, x):
        out = self.conv(x)  # shape: (batch, feature_dim, H, W)
        batch_size, feature_dim, H, W = out.shape
        # Flatten spatially: (seq_length, batch, feature_dim) where seq_length = H*W
        tokens = out.view(batch_size, feature_dim, -1).permute(2, 0, 1)
        return tokens

# Sampler module that uses TokenAcceleration. (For simplicity, we use the same as in Experiment 1)
class AccelerationSampler(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, feature_dim, momentum=0.9):
        super(AccelerationSampler, self).__init__()
        self.token_acceleration = TokenAcceleration(feature_dim, momentum)
    
    def forward(self, token_features, previous_features):
        return self.token_acceleration(token_features, previous_features)

# Composite AFiT model integrating backbone and sampler
class AFiTModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, sampler):
        super(AFiTModel, self).__init__()
        self.backbone = backbone
        self.sampler = sampler

    def forward(self, x, previous_features):
        token_features = self.backbone(x)
        tokens_updated = self.sampler(token_features, previous_features)
        return tokens_updated

def compute_composite_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens, diffusion_loss_weight=1.0, acceleration_loss_weight=1.0):
    # Standard diffusion (denoising) loss: mean squared error
    loss_diffusion = ((predicted_tokens - ground_truth_tokens) ** 2).mean()
    # Secondary loss enforcing consistency (using L1 loss)
    loss_acceleration = nn.functional.l1_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens)
    # Total loss with balancing lambda (acceleration_loss_weight here)
    loss_total = diffusion_loss_weight * loss_diffusion + acceleration_loss_weight * loss_acceleration
    return loss_total

def experiment3():
    print("Beginning Experiment 3: Joint Training of Transformer Backbone and Accelerated Sampler")
    
    # Settings
    batch_size = 2
    image_size = 16  # Use a small image size for demonstration (16x16)
    in_channels = 3
    feature_dim = 32
    num_epochs = 3  # For a quick run
    lambda_acc = 1.0  # weight for secondary loss
    
    # Create dummy dataset: random images and random token targets
    # Input images: shape (batch, in_channels, H, W)
    torch.manual_seed(2)
    images = torch.randn(batch_size, in_channels, image_size, image_size)
    # Ground truth tokens: obtained from backbone ideally, but here we just use random targets
    # For backbone the token shape will be (seq_length, batch, feature_dim)
    seq_length = image_size * image_size  # because of flattening
    ground_truth_tokens = torch.randn(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    
    # Initialize model components
    backbone = DummyBackbone(in_channels, feature_dim)
    sampler = AccelerationSampler(feature_dim, momentum=0.9)
    model = AFiTModel(backbone, sampler)
    model.train()
    
    optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    
    loss_history = []
    # For simplicity, we initialize previous_features as zeros with same shape as tokens.
    previous_features = torch.zeros(seq_length, batch_size, feature_dim)
    
    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        predicted_tokens = model(images, previous_features)
        loss = compute_composite_loss(predicted_tokens, ground_truth_tokens, diffusion_loss_weight=1.0, acceleration_loss_weight=lambda_acc)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        
        loss_val = loss.item()
        loss_history.append(loss_val)
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}/{num_epochs}, Composite Loss: {loss_val:.4f}")
    
    # Plot training loss versus epochs.
    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
    plt.plot(range(1, num_epochs+1), loss_history, marker='o', linestyle='-', color='purple')
    plt.xlabel("Epoch")
    plt.ylabel("Composite Loss")
    plt.title("Joint Training Loss Curve")
    # Save as PDF with naming as required.
    plt.savefig("training_loss_joint_full.pdf")
    plt.close()
    print("Experiment 3 complete. Saved plot as 'training_loss_joint_full.pdf'.\n")


###############################
# Test function to verify the code runs correctly
###############################
def test():
    print("Running tests for all experiments...\n")
    
    start_time = time.time()
    # Run Experiment 1: quick run to test token acceleration module
    experiment1()
    # Run Experiment 2: quick run to test adaptive ODE solver
    experiment2()
    # Run Experiment 3: quick run to test joint training of backbone and sampler
    experiment3()
    
    end_time = time.time()
    print(f"All tests completed in {end_time - start_time:.2f} seconds.")
    
if __name__ == '__main__':
    test()

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Notes:
• The code above uses synthetic data and very few iterations/epochs for testing.
• In practice, you would use real datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, resolution-varied ImageNet) and run much longer training loops.
• All plots are saved explicitly as .pdf files with filenames that meet the naming convention.
• The test() function ensures that the code executes immediately and provides detailed standard output logs.
  
Happy experimenting!
